coredata.dc:description: For the high-value utilization of wood, a novel non-cytotoxic bifunctional wood (NBW) was synthesized in this study for simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pb(II). Adsorption experiments showcased the remarkable maximum adsorption capacity of NBW, measuring 211.6 mg/g. The adsorption isotherm followed the Langmuir model, and concurrently, adsorption kinetics adhered to the pseudo-second-order model. Critically, NBW exhibited exceptional reusability, maintaining 81.0% of its initial adsorption capacity even after undergoing six consecutive adsorption/desorption cycles. Additionally, it demonstrated notable temporal stability, preserving more than 84.2% of its initial adsorption capacity after a fifteen-week storage period. Furthermore, NBW was used for selective and sensitive detection of Pb(II), yielding a low limit of detection of 0.13 μg/L. The fluorescence quenching of NBW resulted from both the internal filtering effect and dynamic quenching. Importantly, the cost analysis demonstrated that the expense of removing Pb(II) per gram using NBW was only CNY 0.50, significantly lower than the cost associated with activated carbon, totaling CNY 2.11. This study lays the groundwork for the development of a long-term stable, reusable, cost-effective, and multifunctional adsorbent.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: For the high-value utilization of wood, a novel non-cytotoxic bifunctional wood (NBW) was synthesized in this study for simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pb(II). Adsorption experiments showcased the remarkable maximum adsorption capacity of NBW, measuring 211.6 mg/g. The adsorption isotherm followed the Langmuir model, and concurrently, adsorption kinetics adhered to the pseudo-second-order model. Critically, NBW exhibited exceptional reusability, maintaining 81.0% of its initial adsorption capacity even after undergoing six consecutive adsorption/desorption cycles. Additionally, it demonstrated notable temporal stability, preserving more than 84.2% of its initial adsorption capacity after a fifteen-week storage period. Furthermore, NBW was used for selective and sensitive detection of Pb(II), yielding a low limit of detection of 0.13 μg/L. The fluorescence quenching of NBW resulted from both the internal filtering effect and dynamic quenching. Importantly, the cost analysis demonstrated that the expense of removing Pb(II) per gram using NBW was only CNY 0.50, significantly lower than the cost associated with activated carbon, totaling CNY 2.11. This study lays the groundwork for the development of a long-term stable, reusable, cost-effective, and multifunctional adsorbent.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ongoing lead contamination constitutes a pervasive environmental threat, exerting widespread impacts on both public health and ecosystems. Historically, a predominant anthropogenic source arises from the extensive use of lead-based paint in both residential and industrial settings. Recent industrial processes, encompassing metal smelting and lead-acid battery manufacturing, have led to substantial lead emissions. Consequently, there is a need for effective treatment of lead pollution. Various techniques have been developed for the removal of Pb(II), including chemical precipitation (Z. Xu et al., 2021), electrodialysis (Huanhuan Tian, 2021), extraction (Sobhi et al., 2019), and adsorption (Jin et al., 2020). Researchers are increasingly interested in the adsorption method due to its efficacy in removing Pb(II). However, developing green and cost-effective adsorbents for Pb(II) continues to pose a significant challenge.

In recent years, nanocellulose has emerged as a promising material. Several high-performance adsorbents have been developed utilizing it as a raw material for the treatment of contaminants such as lead, phosphorus, and chromium (Qiao et al., 2021; Qu et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2023). These investigations have produced favorable outcomes, demonstrating the efficacy of nanocellulose in addressing environmental challenges. The preparation of nanocellulose demands specialized equipment and entails high energy consumption, presenting notable hurdles to its commercialization. To overcome these challenges, an innovative and sustainable approach involves the direct extraction of lignin from wood to obtain nanocellulose, followed by the subsequent preparation of adsorbent. Employing this approach, Zhu et al. (2022) synthesized delignified wood-supported MIL-100 (Fe) to achieve expeditious tetracycline adsorption. Nevertheless, further research is imperative to progress the development of wood-derived adsorbent for Pb(II).

Carbon dots (CDs), one of the carbon-based nanomaterials, have garnered substantial attention since their discovery in 2004. Due to their exceptional chemical and optical properties, particularly fluorescence, CDs find widespread application as fluorescent probes (X. Li et al., 2021). Extensive research supports their efficacy in detecting Pb(II). For instance, Jiang et al. (2022) synthesized phosphorus and chlorine-doped CDs, revealing a linear decrease in fluorescence signal with increasing Pb(II) concentration. Similarly, Radhakrishnan et al. (2019) developed CDs capable of detecting nanomolar levels of Pb(II). Despite these achievements, CDs are susceptible to agglomeration, necessitating a suitable carrier to preserve their fluorescence (Li and Gong, 2022). In this context, a wood-derived adsorbent emerges as a promising carrier.

In summary, we hypothesized that the combination of natural wood (NW) and carbon dots (CDs) could produce an innovative adsorbent termed NBW. To comprehensively characterize the NBW, we employed various analytical techniques, including scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), UV–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy, and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. Subsequently, we investigated the adsorption behavior of NBW towards Pb(II) by examining four key factors: pH, temperature, initial Pb(II) concentration, and contact time. The study revealed the effectiveness of the prepared NBW for detecting Pb(II). Additionally, we discussed the plausible adsorption and detection mechanisms of NBW. Finally, a cost analysis affirmed the economic efficiency of NBW.
All materials and reagents were included in Supplementary material.

The leaching liquor was formulated by blending benzene and ethanol in a 2:1 (v/v) ratio. Subsequently, a NW specimen (Basswood) was immersed in the leaching liquor for 48 h at 80 °C. Following this, a rinsing procedure with distilled water was executed. The treated specimen was then immersed in a 5.8 wt% solution of NaClO2 under acetic acid condition (pH=4.7) and boiled at 90 °C for 15 h. After this treatment, the specimen was submerged in a H2O2 solution (2.5 mol/L, 20 mL) at 90 °C for 2 h. It was subsequently cleansed using ethanol and distilled water, and named as delignified wood (DW).

1.0 g of betaine hydrochloride, 0.32 g of sulfadiazine, and 0.3 mL of ethylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL of distilled water and subjected to a 30-minute ultrasonication process. The resulting mixture was transferred to a 150 mL hydrothermal reactor, undergoing heating at 180 °C for 4 h. Following the thermal treatment, the reactor underwent a natural cooling process. The resulting solution underwent filtration to remove insoluble solid particles, facilitating the formation of the target product, CDs. A TEM image of these CDs was presented in Fig. S1.

A DW specimen and a 20 mL solution of CDs at a concentration of 0.04 wt% were combined in a wide-mouth bottle. Vacuum filtration lasted for 30 min. Subsequently, the CDs solution was replaced, and an additional 30-minute vacuum filtration was performed. After five cycles, the treated specimen was washed through three rinses with distilled water, followed by immersion in absolute ethanol for 8 h. After ethanol immersion, the specimen was placed in a gel solution containing 0.065 g of N′, N-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA), 6.0 g of acrylic acid (AA), 14.2 mL of 5 mol/L NaOH solution, and 0.03 g of potassium persulfate (PPS). Vacuum filtration lasted for 30 min, and then the gel solution was replaced, followed by an additional 30-min vacuum filtration. After five cycles, the mixture was heated at 60 °C for 12 h. The obtained product was cleaned with ethanol and distilled water and designated as NBW-3. The resulting products were named NBW-1 to NBW-8, representing concentrations of 0.02 wt%, 0.03 wt%, 0.04 wt%, 0.05 wt%, 0.07 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, and 0.4 wt%, respectively.

The influence of pH on adsorption was examined within the pH range of 1 to 5, maintaining a constant temperature of 30 °C. Similarly, the impact of temperature on adsorption behavior was investigated over the temperature range of 20 °C to 60 °C. Adsorption isotherms were generated at initial concentrations varying from 20 to 120 mg/L while maintaining a pH of 5. Kinetic studies on adsorption involved experiments conducted over a contact time range of 30 to 240 min, with residual Pb(II) concentrations measured at specific intervals. To evaluate reusability, experiments were conducted using 0.01 mol/L nitric acid (100 mL) as the eluent. Atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) was employed to analyze both initial and final solution concentrations. Each experiment was replicated three times, and if the deviation exceeded 5%, additional trials were conducted to ensure accuracy.

Microscopic morphological structure was analyzed using SEM (JSM-7500 F, JEOL, Japan). FTIR spectroscopy was conducted with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iN10 instrument (United States) across the spectral range of 4000 cm⁻¹ to 500 cm⁻¹ , employing attenuated total reflectance mode for investigating functional groups. Crystalline phases were identified through XRD analysis, utilizing the Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Germany). PL spectra were recorded on an F3000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–visible absorption spectra were measured using a Lambda 950 UV spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA).

Fig. 1 depicts the preparation process of NBW, highlighting the significance of NW and CDs as crucial components. Delignification played a pivotal role in eliminating lignin, enhancing nanopores (Zhang et al., 2023), thereby increasing adsorption sites and specific surface area. Vacuum infiltration facilitated easy dispersion of CDs within the DW. Subsequently, the introduction of a gel solution assisted in forming a 3D porous structure of the NBW (Ma et al., 2022).

SEM was employed to evaluate the morphological characteristics of NW, DW, and NBW-3. The NW exhibited an inherent 3D porous structure, as illustrated in Fig. 2a–d. In the case of DW, the delamination of cell walls occurred, revealing many nanopores (Fig. 2e–h). This phenomenon resulted from the partial removal of lignin, leading to the emergence of distinct lumens along the direction of wood growth, while preserving the original cellulose skeleton structure. In the context of NBW-3, a reconstructed 3D porous structure was observed, accompanied by the transformation of the cell wall's rough surface to a smoother texture (Fig. 2i–l). Notably, all channels and pores of NBW were effectively filled with polymer chains, enhancing the material's capacity for Pb(II) adsorption.

Fig. 3a illustrates the XRD patterns of NW, DW, and NBW-3. Notably, two distinct peaks emerged at 16.2° and 22.2° (Yuan et al., 2021), corresponding to the (100) and (002) crystal planes of cellulose, respectively. The calculated crystalline indices for these three samples were 1.56, 1.96, and 2.09, respectively (Chen et al., 2020). The elevated crystalline index of DW, in comparison to NW, suggested a higher degree of crystallinity, potentially attributed to the removal of amorphous lignin (De et al., 2020). Additionally, NBW-3 exhibited a further heightened crystalline index compared to DW, signifying the reconstruction of a 3D porous structure (Ran and Yang, 2022).

Fig. 3b illustrates the FTIR spectra of NW, DW, and NBW-3. In the NW spectrum, two peaks at 1592 and 1505 cm−1 represented the stretching vibrations of aromatic rings in lignin (Guo et al., 2022). These distinctive peaks disappear in DW, affirming the efficacious removal of lignin. Both NW and DW spectra exhibited a peak at 1050 cm−1 (Khadir et al., 2021), corresponding to the C–O stretching vibration of cellulose. In the case of NBW-3, a diminished peak at 1050 cm−1 indicated the involvement of cellulose in the free-radical polymerization reaction. Furthermore, the presence of a peak at 1650 cm−1 confirmed the –CO–NH– chemical bond (Wu et al., 2019), indicating the successful utilization of MBA as a cross-linking agent in the synthesis of NBW-3.

Fig. 3c displays a notable UV–vis absorption peak at 346 nm in the spectrum of NBW-3. This peak was likely attributed to the n-π * electron transition (Thu-Huyen et al., 2019). Furthermore, In the PL spectrum, NBW-3 had optimal excitation and emission wavelengths at 395 nm and 475 nm, separately. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 3d, NBW-3 presented excitation-dependent PL emission peaks, closely resembling the characteristic features of CDs (Xu et al., 2022).

Fig. 3e illustrates the fluorescence characteristics of NBW-x (x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, and 8). Discrepancies in fluorescence behavior primarily arose from variations in the content of CDs. The morphological features of NBW-x were assessed by examining their cross-sections using SEM, as depicted in Fig. S2. Notably, among these samples, NBW-3 exhibited the most intense fluorescence emission, accompanied by an absolute quantum yield (AQY0) of 17.5%. The procedure for determining AQY was detailed in the Supplementary Material. Cross-sectional analyses of NBW-3 revealed consistent fluorescence throughout its structure (Figs. 3f and 3g), confirming a uniform distribution of CDs.

Temperature, pH, and initial concentration (C0) played pivotal roles in influencing the equilibrium adsorption capacity (Qe) of NBW-3. The intricate interplay among these factors is visually represented in the 3D response surface plots ( Fig. 4a–c) (Uddin and Nasar, 2020).

The pH significantly influenced the adsorption of Pb(II), as depicted in Fig. 4d. In the pH range of 1–5, the Qe of NBW-3 consistently increased. Favorable adsorption of a positively charged pollutant occurs when the pH exceeds the point of zero charge (pzc) of the adsorbent (Li et al., 2022). Here, the pzc of NBW-3 was determined to be 4.4 (Fig. S3). Consequently, when the solution pH exceeded 4.4, NBW-3 demonstrated an enhanced Qe. Additionally, the divalent form of the lead ion prevails at pH values below 6 (Jalu et al., 2021). Therefore, a pH of 5 was used in the subsequent experiments.

Fig. 4e depicts the variation in the Qe of NBW-3 at different temperatures (20 ℃, 30 ℃, 40 ℃, 50 ℃, and 60 ℃). The Qe of NBW-3 exhibited a gradual increase with ascending temperature, aligning with established trends observed in prior research on wood-based adsorbents (Q. Wang et al., 2021). For practical convenience, a temperature of 30 ℃ was chosen.

Adsorption isotherms were constructed for NW, DW, and NBW-3 at various initial concentrations, as depicted in Fig. 4 f. As C0 increased, the adsorption capacities of these samples progressively rose. Notably, at equivalent C0 level, NBW-3 exhibited a superior Qe compared to DW and NW, highlighting the beneficial influence of CDs in Pb(II) adsorption (Wang et al., 2023). The adsorption isotherm of NBW-3 was fitted using Langmuir, Freundlich, Temkin, and Dubinin–Radushkevich (D–R) models (Alqadami et al., 2020), as illustrated in Fig. 4 h, 4i, S4, and S5, respectively. Table S1 details the adsorption isotherm parameters, with NBW-3 displaying a maximum adsorption capacity (Qmax) of 211.6 mg/g. In contrast to other absorbents documented in the literature (refer to Table 1), NBW-3 showcased a significantly higher adsorption ability. Therefore, within our specific context, NBW-3 demonstrates the potential to serve as an efficient absorbent for Pb(II) (Fan et al., 2022).

Fig. 4 g illustrates the impact of contact time on the adsorption process. Initially, the adsorption capacity showed a rapid increase, followed by a gradual rise until reaching equilibrium. NBW-3 displayed a superior adsorption capacity compared to DW and NW, confirming the positive influence of CDs on Pb(II) adsorption (Wang et al., 2023). The kinetics of NBW-3 were thoroughly evaluated using the pseudo-first-order (PFO) and pseudo-second-order (PSO) models, as presented in Figs. 4j and 4k. Table S2 provides the adsorption kinetics parameters. The PSO model exhibited superior accuracy in terms of correlation coefficient (R2), indicating that the adsorption process of NBW-3 primarily involved chemical adsorption (Xiong et al., 2020).

The reusability of NBW-3 was assessed over six consecutive adsorption-desorption cycles, depicted in Fig. 5a. After six cycles, NBW-3 maintained an adsorption capacity of 15.26 mg/g, equivalent to 81.0% of its initial adsorption capacity. The SEM image (Fig. S6) revealed that the structural morphology of NBW-3 after six cycles closely resembled that of the as-synthesized NBW-3, retaining the 3D porous structure. Additionally, as illustrated in Fig. 5b, NBW-3 preserved over 84.2% of its initial adsorption capacity for fifteen weeks. Importantly, the crystal structure and chemical composition of NBW-3 remained intact after reuse and storage, as confirmed by FTIR (Figs. 5c and 5d) and XRD (Figs. 5e and 5f) analyses. These findings suggest that NBW-3 is suitable as a long-term stable and reusable absorbent (Imanipoor et al., 2021).

An investigation was conducted to evaluate the efficacy of NBW-3 in removing Pb(II) from three authentic water samples, as depicted in Fig. S7. The key quality parameters of these water samples are summarized in Table S3. Among them, tap water exhibited the highest Qe, while laboratory wastewater displayed the lowest. It was noteworthy that the Qe of NBW-3 in these authentic water samples was lower than in standard solutions. This decrease was attributed to the competition among Pb(II), other heavy metals, and organic constituents for available sorption sites on NBW-3 (Liu et al., 2022). Despite encountering several adverse factors, it is worth emphasizing that NBW-3 demonstrates effective adsorption ability for Pb(II) in authentic water samples.

To assess the selectivity of the synthesized NBW-3 toward Pb(II), the fluorescence response to various metal ions was investigated ( Fig. 6a). The tested metal ions, including Zn2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Na+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Ni2+, K+, Fe2+, Cu2+, and Pb2+, were each present at a concentration of 2000 mg/L. Remarkably, Pb(II) induced the most significant fluorescence quenching, while other metal ions had minimal impact on the fluorescence of NBW-3. Furthermore, the absolute quantum yield after introducing different metal ions (AQY) was measured. The change in AQY/AQY0 of NBW-3 unequivocally demonstrated that Pb(II) was the sole metal ion responsible for the fluorescence quenching, as depicted in Fig. 6b. These findings confirmed that NBW-3 exhibited notable selectivity for Pb(II). Subsequently, the anti-interference capabilities of the NBW-3/Pb(II) system were comprehensively examined. Various coexisting metal ions, including Zn2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Ba2+, Na+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Al3+, Ni2+, K+, Fe2+, and Cu2+, were investigated. These metal ions induced minimal changes in fluorescence quenching, as illustrated in Figs. 7a and 7b. Notably, among the tested metal ions, Pb(II) exhibited the most significant responsiveness (Sharma and Mehata, 2020). This observation substantiated the robust anti-interference capacity of NBW-3 (Song et al., 2019).

The experimental parameters, including temperature, detection time, and pH value, were systematically optimized. In Fig. S8, the fluorescence intensity of NBW-3 remained stable at temperatures up to 60 °C, indicating its resilience to temperature fluctuations. Upon the introduction of Pb(II), NBW-3's fluorescence response increased with rising temperature (Li et al., 2020). For experimental convenience, a temperature of 30 °C was chosen. Fig. S9 shows the rapid initial increase in NBW-3's fluorescence response to Pb(II) within the first 18 min, followed by stabilization, leading to the determination of an optimal detection time of 18 min. Additionally, Fig. S10 demonstrates that adjusting the pH from 1 to 5 increased NBW-3's fluorescence intensity, with the peak response occurring at a pH of 5. Consequently, pH 5 was identified as the optimal pH condition.

Under optimized conditions, the sensitivity of NBW-3 to detect Pb(II) was assessed. The AQY of NBW-3 exhibited an initial sharp decrease followed by a gradual decline upon incremental addition of Pb(II), as depicted in Fig. 8a. To quantify the AQY, we utilized the equation AQY = –0.0086 ×C0 + 17.27. Additionally, the PL intensity quenching of NBW-3 with increasing Pb(II) concentration was observed (Fig. 8b). A good linear relationship between I0/I–1 and Pb(II) concentration in the range from 0.2 to 1.0 μg/L was obtained. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.13 μg/L (3 s/k) (Y. Liu et al., 2020). In comparison to other fluorescent probes documented in the literature ( Table 2), NBW-3 demonstrated a significantly superior LOD and a broader linear range.

To evaluate the applicability of NBW-3, authentic water samples, comprising tap water and lake water, were collected for Pb(II) detection. In spiked tests, suitable amounts of Pb(II) were added to the authentic water samples. Table S4 presents the recoveries, ranging from 99.1% to 101.7%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) below 3.1%, affirming the suitability of NBW-3 for authentic water samples.

NIH/3T3 cells were exposed to varying concentrations of NBW-3, and a CCK-8 cell viability assay was conducted (Liu et al., 2019). The cell viability results ( Fig. 9a) revealed minimal cytotoxicity (92%) for both NBW-3 and NW, even at the high concentration of 1000 μg/mL. Live–dead staining of NIH/3T3 cells in NBW-3 (Fig. 9b) showed live cells emitting blue fluorescence, while dead cells emitted red fluorescence. Remarkably, the images showed almost no visible dead cells. These findings highlighted the remarkably low cytotoxicity of NBW-3 (Wan et al., 2019), making it well-suited for water treatment.

The adsorption mechanism of NBW-3 was explored using FTIR spectroscopy. In Fig. 10a, the peak intensities of O−H and N − H stretching vibrations at 3400 cm−1 decreased (Keirudin et al., 2020), and the –COO– peak at 1562 cm−1 exhibited reduced intensity (Kong et al., 2019). These observations underscore the effectiveness of –OH, –NH2, and –COO– groups in Pb(II) adsorption (Wang et al., 2022).

To gain deeper insights into the adsorption mechanism of NBW-3, XPS spectra were analyzed (Fig. 10b). The presence of Pb4f in the XPS spectra confirmed effective Pb(II) adsorption. Concurrently, the decrease in the Na peak, coupled with a significant increase in the Pb peak, provided clear evidence of ion exchange (Lin et al., 2023). The high-resolution Pb4f spectrum (Fig. 10c) exhibited two prominent peaks at 142.98 eV and 138.18 eV, corresponding to Pb4f5/2 and Pb4f7/2 (F. Liu et al., 2020), respectively, further confirming the adsorption of Pb(II) onto NBW-3. In Fig. 10d, the O1s XPS spectrum displayed two peaks: C–O at 531.98 eV and CO at 530.38 eV (Zhang et al., 2022). Post-adsorption, the C–O peak shifted from 531.98 to 532.18 eV due to electrostatic attraction between –COO– and Pb(II) (W. Li et al., 2021). The high-resolution N1s spectrum of NBW-3 prior to Pb(II) adsorption displayed two peaks at binding energies of 401.28 eV (–NH3 +) and 399.21 eV (–NH2) (Fig. 10e). After adsorption, the –NH2 peak shifted to 399.29 eV, indicating the complexation between –NH2 and Pb(II) (Miao et al., 2021).

We scrutinized the detection mechanism of NBW-3 by examining UV–vis absorption and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra. In Fig. 11a, Pb(II) displayed a UV–vis absorption peak at 395 nm, coinciding with NBW-3's PL excitation spectrum, confirming the presence of an inner filter effect (Yan et al., 2020). Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of NBW-3 was estimated. As depicted in Fig. 11b–d, NBW-3 exhibited an average fluorescence lifetime of 1.41 ns without Pb(II) and 0.36 ns in its presence. This reduction in fluorescence lifetime provided evidence for the occurrence of dynamic quenching (Chini et al., 2019).

Fig. 12 illustrates the adsorption and detection of Pb(II) by NBW-3. Initially, Pb(II) adhered to the surface of NBW-3 through electrostatic forces. Subsequently, these ions permeated the interior of NBW-3, benefiting from its reconstructed 3D porous structure, which provided additional adsorption sites and thereby enhanced the adsorption capacity of NBW-3. Simultaneously, numerous Pb(II) were adsorbed through ion exchange, electrostatic interaction, and complexation mechanisms. Additionally, the presence of CDs bolstered Pb(II) adsorption through carboxyl groups. Consequently, fluorescence quenching occurred in NBW-3, enabling the detection of Pb(II) through the internal filtering effect and dynamic quenching.

Economic analysis was conducted to assess the feasibility of NBW-3 as an adsorbent. The total cost of NBW-3 amounted to CNY 106.18/kg, significantly lower than that of traditional adsorbents such as activated carbon (CNY 253/kg) (Table S5). Cost comparisons for removing 1 g of Pb(II) using NBW-3 and activated carbon, considering their respective adsorption capacities, are detailed in Table S6. The results indicated that Pb(II) removal with NBW-3 incurred a cost of CNY 0.50/g, representing only 24% of the expense associated with activated carbon. Thus, NBW-3 emerges as a cost-effective adsorbent for Pb(II) removal.

NBW was synthesized for efficient Pb(II) adsorption and sensitive detection, showcasing long-term stability, reusability, cost-effectiveness, and bifunctionality. Adsorption experiments revealed NBW's impressive maximum sorption capacity of 211.6 mg/g. Over fifteen weeks and six cycles, NBW retained 84.2% and 81.0% of its initial adsorption capacity, respectively. Notably, NBW demonstrated linear detection ranges for Pb(II): 5 to 400 mg/L and 0.2 to 1.0 μg/L, with an LOD of 0.13 μg/L. Cost analysis underscored NBW's superior cost-effectiveness compared to activated carbon. This study lays the foundation for the development of stable and cost-effective adsorbent.

Ma Qiang: Investigation. Zuo Yingfeng: Resources. Zhang Xuefeng: Investigation. Liu Meng: Software. Chen Yizheng: Software. Qi Xinmiao: Investigation. Wu Yiqiang: Resources. Guo Xin: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xie Xiangjing: Resources.
